_note: Anna translation drill — initial 15 exercises for Phase 2B Coach tab MVP. Authored 2026-04-30. Distribution targets her actual measured weak_patterns from coach_notes (Grammar mechanics 44%, Vocabulary production 37%, Collocations 22%, Everyday English idioms) plus 4 prepositions per design-intent profile. Themes: home, padel, neighbours, daily life. Push to exercises_library/translation/{id} via tools/push_library.js (to be built in Phase 2B).

exercises.source_observation: Profile-documented L1 swap: 'wait us' instead of 'wait for us'
exercises.prompt_ru: Соседи ждут нас на ужин в субботу.
correct_answers: The neighbours are waiting for us for dinner on Saturday
The neighbours are waiting for us for dinner on Saturday.
Our neighbours are waiting for us for dinner on Saturday
The neighbors are waiting for us for dinner on Saturday
The neighbours are expecting us for dinner on Saturday

common_errors.feedback: Russian 'ждут нас' takes no preposition, but English 'wait' requires 'for'. Compare: 'expect us' (no preposition) vs 'wait for us' (with). Both fit here — pick one. ✗ 'wait us' is the most common Russian-speaker preposition slip.

common_errors.pattern_label: wrong preposition with day of week
common_errors.feedback: Days of the week take 'on' in English: 'on Saturday', 'on Monday'. ✗ 'in Saturday' / 'at Saturday' — Russian 'в субботу' uses one preposition for everything; English splits: 'on' for days, 'in' for months/years, 'at' for clock times.

Use the present progressive for an action happening now or arranged for a near future event: 'are waiting'. ✗ 'The neighbours wait' (simple) suggests a habit. Russian present tense 'ждут' covers both meanings — English splits them.

exercises.fallback_feedback: That doesn't match. The target uses 'wait for' (with the preposition) or 'expect' (no preposition), in the present progressive ('are waiting' / 'are expecting'), with 'on Saturday' for the day.

exercises.source_observation: Profile-documented L1 swap: 'arrive to' instead of 'arrive at/in'
exercises.prompt_ru: Мы приехали на корт раньше всех.
correct_answers: We arrived at the court before everyone else
We arrived at the court earlier than everyone else
We got to the court before everyone else
We got to the court earlier than everyone else

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'arrive to' is the single most common Russian-speaker preposition error. English uses 'arrive at' (a specific place — court, station, airport) or 'arrive in' (a city/country). Russian 'приехать в/на' maps to neither. Alternative verb: 'get to' takes 'to' but it's a different verb.
Russian 'раньше всех' translates as 'before everyone' or 'earlier than everyone', not 'before all'. ✗ 'before all' sounds biblical/archaic. Natural: 'before everyone (else)'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target verb is 'arrive at the court' or 'get to the court'. Russian 'раньше всех' = 'before everyone (else)' or 'earlier than everyone (else)'.

correct_answers: It depends on the weather
That depends on the weather
It depends on the weather.

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'depend from' is a direct Russian calque (зависеть от). English fixes the preposition: 'depend on'. Always 'on', never 'from' or 'of'.
✗ 'depend of' is a French/Spanish-style error. English requires 'depend on'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Two-word target: 'depends on'. The fixed preposition for 'depend' is always 'on'.

exercises.source_observation: Profile-documented L1 swap: 'at the next week' instead of zero-preposition 'next week'
exercises.prompt_ru: На следующей неделе мы идём в гости к Светлане.
correct_answers: Next week we are going to Svetlana's
We are going to Svetlana's next week
Next week we are visiting Svetlana
We are visiting Svetlana next week
Next week we are going to visit Svetlana
We are going to visit Svetlana next week

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'on/in/at next week' — English uses NO preposition with 'next/this/last + time-word': 'next week', 'last Monday', 'this morning'. Russian 'на следующей неделе' has a preposition; English drops it. Just say 'next week'.
✗ Russian 'идти в гости' has no direct English equivalent. Use 'visit someone', 'go to someone's' (apostrophe + s = their place), or 'go to see someone'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: No preposition before 'next week'. For 'идти в гости к Светлане', say 'going to Svetlana's' (= her place) or 'visiting Svetlana'.

exercises.source_observation: coach_notes weak_pattern: Grammar mechanics — tag questions 44% over n=63
correct_answers: You like padel, don't you?
You love padel, don't you?
You do like padel, don't you?

common_errors.feedback: Tag questions REVERSE the polarity of the main clause. Positive statement → negative tag: 'You like padel, don't you?'. ✗ 'You like padel, do you?' would only work as a surprised reaction ('Oh, you like padel, do you?').
Russian 'правда?' / 'ведь' marks a tag question — English uses an inverted auxiliary at the end: 'don't you?', 'right?', 'aren't you?'. The form depends on the main verb's tense and auxiliary.

✗ 'isn't it?' only works when the subject is 'it' or impersonal. With 'you' as subject, the tag uses 'do/don't': 'You like padel, don't you?'. Russian 'правда?' is one universal tag — English picks the auxiliary from the main verb.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target structure: positive main clause + reversed-polarity tag. Main verb 'like' (present simple) → tag 'don't you?'. Full: 'You like padel, don't you?'.

exercises.source_observation: coach_notes weak_pattern: past simple irregulars within Grammar mechanics 44%
exercises.prompt_ru: Я купила новую посуду и принесла её домой вчера.
correct_answers: I bought new dishes and brought them home yesterday
I bought some new dishes and brought them home yesterday
I bought new tableware and brought it home yesterday
I bought new crockery and brought it home yesterday

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'buy' and 'bring' are irregular: past = 'bought' / 'brought'. Both rhyme but spell differently. Russian regular '-л' ending ('купила', 'принесла') tempts learners to add '-ed' — but these verbs don't take it.
Russian 'посуда' is singular (uncountable) but refers to a set. English options: 'dishes' (plural countable), 'tableware' / 'crockery' (uncountable). ✗ 'a new dish' = one specific plate or food item.
Coordinated past actions both need past tense: 'bought ... and brought'. ✗ 'bought ... and bring' mixes tenses. Russian 'купила и принесла' is consistent — keep that consistency in English.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'bought' (irregular past of buy) and 'brought' (irregular past of bring). For 'посуда' use plural 'dishes' or uncountable 'tableware'.

exercises.source_observation: coach_notes weak_pattern: agreement within Grammar mechanics 44%
exercises.prompt_ru: У моей сестры трое детей, и они все ходят в одну школу.
correct_answers: My sister has three children and they all go to the same school
My sister has three kids and they all go to the same school
My sister has three children, and they all go to the same school
My sister has three kids and they all attend the same school

common_errors.feedback: ✗ Third-person singular requires '-s': 'My sister HAS' (not 'have'). Russian 'у сестры есть' has no agreement — English makes you mark it on every present-simple verb.
✗ Russian 'у моей сестры' has no English equivalent preposition. Restructure with 'have/has': 'My sister has...'. The Russian 'possessor + у' pattern always becomes English 'subject + have/has'.
✗ 'they' is plural — verb is 'go' (no -s). Subject-verb agreement: 'he/she/it + verb-s', everyone else + base form.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'My sister HAS three children/kids and they all GO to the same school'. Watch the third-person 's' on 'has' and the bare plural 'go'.

exercises.source_observation: coach_notes weak_pattern: vocabulary production 37% on input — common-word recall
exercises.prompt_ru: Мне нужно полить цветы и пропылесосить ковёр.
correct_answers: I need to water the flowers and vacuum the carpet
I need to water the plants and vacuum the carpet
I have to water the flowers and vacuum the carpet
I need to water the flowers and hoover the carpet
I need to water the plants and hoover the carpet

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'pour the flowers' — Russian 'полить' translates as the verb 'water' (the noun water becomes a verb): 'water the plants'. 'Pour' takes a liquid as object: 'pour water', not 'pour flowers'.
'Clean' is too generic. The specific verb for using a vacuum cleaner is 'vacuum' (US/global) or 'hoover' (UK informal, from the brand). ✗ 'pylesos' has no English cognate; 'vacuum' is the verb.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Two specific verbs needed: 'water the flowers/plants' (полить = use water as verb) and 'vacuum the carpet' (пропылесосить = vacuum as verb).

exercises.prompt_ru: Мы выиграли у соседей со счётом 6:3.
correct_answers: We beat the neighbours 6-3
We beat our neighbours 6-3
We beat the neighbours six-three
We won against the neighbours 6-3
We won 6-3 against the neighbours
We beat the neighbors 6-3
We beat the neighbours by 6-3

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'won the neighbours' is wrong — 'win' takes a thing (won the match, won a prize) but a person needs 'against' or you switch to 'beat'. Russian 'выиграть у' maps best to: 'beat someone' (no preposition) or 'win against someone'.
Russian 'со счётом 6:3' translates simply as the score itself: '6-3' (six-three). ✗ 'with the score 6:3' is a literal calque — English drops the framing.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target verb: 'beat' (no preposition) or 'win against'. Score: just '6-3', no 'with the score' wrapper.

exercises.prompt_ru: Этот диван очень удобный, и время очень удобное для встречи.
correct_answers: This sofa is very comfortable and the time is very convenient for the meeting
This sofa is very comfortable, and the time is very convenient for the meeting
This sofa is really comfortable and the time is really convenient for the meeting
This sofa is very comfortable and the time is convenient for the meeting

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'comfortable time' — Russian 'удобный' covers BOTH meanings, but English splits: 'comfortable' (physical comfort: chair, bed, clothes) vs 'convenient' (suits the schedule, easy to access). A time/place/schedule is 'convenient'. A sofa is 'comfortable'.

✗ 'convenient sofa' — physical objects you sit/lie on are 'comfortable' (gives physical ease), not 'convenient' (suits planning/schedule). Russian 'удобный' is one word; English needs you to pick which sense.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Russian 'удобный' splits in English: 'comfortable' (physical — sofa, shoes, bed) vs 'convenient' (timing, location, access). Sofa = comfortable; time = convenient.

exercises.prompt_ru: Мне нужно принять решение до конца недели.
correct_answers: I need to make a decision by the end of the week
I have to make a decision by the end of the week
I need to make a decision before the end of the week
I need to come to a decision by the end of the week
I need to reach a decision by the end of the week

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'take a decision' — direct calque of Russian 'принять решение'. Standard English collocation is 'MAKE a decision' (or 'reach/come to a decision' for the deliberative sense). 'Take' + decision sounds like translated text.

✗ 'until the end of the week' = continuously up to that point ('I'll be away UNTIL Friday'). For a deadline, use 'BY': 'by the end of the week' = no later than that point. Russian 'до' covers both — English splits.
✗ 'do a decision' — wrong delexical verb. The fixed collocation is 'make a decision'. Mnemonic: you MAKE choices and decisions; you DO tasks and chores.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Two collocations: 'MAKE a decision' (not 'take' or 'do') and 'BY the end of the week' (deadline, not 'until').

exercises.prompt_ru: Дочка делает уроки, а я готовлю ужин.
correct_answers: My daughter is doing her homework and I am cooking dinner
My daughter is doing her homework while I am cooking dinner
My daughter is doing her homework, and I am cooking dinner
My daughter is doing homework and I am making dinner
My daughter is doing her homework and I am making dinner

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'make homework' — direct calque of 'делать уроки'. English uses 'DO homework'. The MAKE/DO split: MAKE for things you create (a cake, a decision, a plan); DO for tasks/chores (homework, the dishes, the laundry).
✗ 'homeworks' — 'homework' is uncountable in English, no plural -s. Russian 'уроки' looks plural but English keeps 'homework' as a mass noun.

✗ 'doing dinner' — for meals use 'cook dinner' or 'make dinner', not 'do dinner'. The DO/MAKE/COOK split: cook (heat, prepare with a stove) and make (any food preparation including assembly) both fit; 'do' does not.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Two collocations: 'DO homework' (not 'make') and 'COOK/MAKE dinner' (not 'do'). 'Homework' is uncountable — no plural.

exercises.source_observation: coach_notes weak_pattern: Everyday English idiomatic chunks (rain check)
exercises.prompt_ru: Извини, сегодня не получится — давай перенесём?
correct_answers: Sorry, today won't work — can I take a rain check?
Sorry, today won't work — can we take a rain check?
Sorry, today doesn't work — can I take a rain check?
Sorry, today won't work — let's reschedule
Sorry, today doesn't work — can we reschedule?
Sorry, I can't make it today — can I take a rain check?
Sorry, today is no good — can we reschedule?

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'transfer it' is a Russian-style calque of 'перенести'. For appointments/plans, English uses 'reschedule' (formal) or 'take a rain check' (informal — declining now but agreeing to do it later). 'Transfer' = move money or files.

common_errors.pattern_label: don't get instead of won't work
common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'I don't get' = 'I don't understand' in English. For Russian 'не получится' meaning 'it's not going to work out', say: 'today won't work', 'I can't make it', 'today doesn't work for me'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Two natural phrasings: 'today won't work' (or 'I can't make it today') for 'не получится', and 'take a rain check' (informal) or 'reschedule' (neutral) for 'давай перенесём'.

exercises.source_observation: coach_notes weak_pattern: Everyday English idiomatic chunks (getting on)
exercises.prompt_ru: Как у вас дела с новой соседкой?
correct_answers: How are you getting on with the new neighbour?
How are you getting on with your new neighbour?
How are you getting along with the new neighbour?
How are you getting along with your new neighbour?
How are you getting on with the new neighbor?
How are things with the new neighbour?
How are things going with the new neighbour?

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'How are your things/affairs' — Russian 'Как у вас дела с' has no direct English equivalent. Idiomatic options: 'How are you getting on/along with X?' (relationship), 'How are things with X?' (general), 'How is it going with X?'.
✗ Missing 'with' — the construction is 'get on/along WITH someone'. 'Getting on with the new neighbour' (relationship). Without 'with', 'get on' = board a vehicle (get on the bus).

exercises.fallback_feedback: Idiomatic: 'How are you getting on/along WITH the new neighbour?' or 'How are things with the new neighbour?'. The phrasal verb 'get on with someone' = have a good relationship.

exercises.source_observation: coach_notes weak_pattern: Everyday English idiomatic chunks (hop on/in)
exercises.prompt_ru: Запрыгивай в машину, я тебя подвезу до корта.
correct_answers: Hop in, I'll give you a lift to the court
Hop in the car, I'll give you a lift to the court
Hop in the car, I'll drive you to the court
Jump in, I'll give you a lift to the court
Hop in, I'll drop you off at the court
Hop in, I'll drive you to the court
Hop in the car — I'll give you a lift to the court

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'jump into the car' is literal but non-idiomatic. Native phrasing: 'hop in (the car)' / 'jump in' — informal, friendly, with NO 'into'. 'Hop in' is the standard chunk for inviting someone into your vehicle.

✗ 'bring you to the court' — Russian 'подвезти' is a phrasal-verb meaning. English options: 'give you a lift', 'drive you', 'drop you off'. 'Bring' implies carrying a thing; 'carry' implies in your arms.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Two informal chunks: 'hop in (the car)' for 'запрыгивай' (no 'into'), and 'give you a lift' / 'drive you' / 'drop you off' for 'подвезти'.
